The INOR-8 surveillance specimens and components from the MSRE that had been exposed to fuel salt formed shallow intergranular cracks (2 to 10 mils deep in exposures to greater than 20,000 hr).
It operated at temperatures around $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for more than 20,000 hr between 1965 and December, 1969.
Except for the graphite, all parts contacting the salt were of a nickel-base alloy known as INOR-8 and now available commercially under the trade names of Hastelloy N and Allvac N.
This alloy, developed at Oak Ridge National Laboratory specifically for use in fluoride salts at high temperature, $^{2}$ has the nominal composition of Ni-16% Mo-7% Cr-5% Fe-0.05% C.
INOR-8 in the MSRE behaved as expected with regard to corrosion by the fluoride salts and the containment atmosphere (very little by either).
Two problems with INOR-8 did appear, however.
The second was the appearance of grain-boundary cracks at INOR-8 surfaces exposed to the fuel salt.
Embrittlement phenomena have been studied extensively for the past several years in connection with various iron- and nickel-base alloys<sup>3</sup> and specifically with regard to INOR-8 by ORNL.
The embrittlement of INOR-8 in the MSRE has been attributed mainly to the helium that is generated by thermal neutron interaction with $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ present in the alloy as an impurity.

We have found that small changes in chemical composition are quite effective in reducing the effects of helium production, and our work is well along toward developing a modified INOR-8 with improved resistance to embrittlement by neutron irradiation.
This work has been reported extensively<sup>4-11</sup> and will be discussed in this document only insofar as it relates to the finding and interpretation of evidence on the surface cracking problem.

every INOR-8 surface that was examined after prolonged contact with the radioactive fuel salt; few or none could be found on INOR-8 surfaces (sometimes on the same piece) that had been exposed to the fluoride salt in the coolant system or to the containment cell atmosphere.
The depth of cracking was 2 to 10 mils, and some sectioned specimens showed as many as 300 cracks per inch of edge.
A good, general description of the MSRE and an account of most of its history appear in reference 1.
Reference 12 is a detailed description of all components and systems.
Because these references are widely available, the description here is confined to those portions that are germane to the discussion of the INOR-8 cracking.
The cracking phenomenon was observed on pieces of INOR-8 from the reactor vessel, the heat exchanger, the fuel pump and a freeze valve near fuel drain tank No.
INOR-8 specimens exposed to the containment cell atmosphere outside the reactor vessel and surfaces exposed to the coolant salt were examined but did not show the cracking.
The fuel salt composition was $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ (65-30-5-<1 mole $\%$ ); the coolant, $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2}$ (66-34 mole $\%$ ).

At full power the 1200-gpm fuel stream normally entered the reactor vessel at $632^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and left at $654^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; the maximum outlet temperature at which the reactor operated for any substantial period of time was $663^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (1225°F).
When the reactor was at low power, the salt systems were usually nearly isothermal at about $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The liquidus temperature of the fuel salt was about $440^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and that of the coolant salt was $459^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , so the loops were heated to $600 - 650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with external electric heaters before the salt was transferred from the storage tanks.
The pump bowl served as the surge space for the loop and also for separation of gaseous fission products from a 50-gpm stream of salt sprayed out into the gas space above the salt pool.
To protect the sample bucket from the salt spray in the pump bowl, a spiral baffle of INOR-8 extended from the top of the bowl down into the salt pool.
A cage of INOR-8 rods inside the spiral baffle guided the sample capsule in the pump bowl.
In the lattice near the center of the core three thimbles of INOR-8 housed control rods.
This array was designed to expose surveillance specimens of graphite and INOR-8 identical to the material used in the MSRE core and reactor vessel.
Later, specimens of modified INOR-8 were included.
Each stringer included a column of graphite specimens and two rods of INOR-8.

Construction had been finished and salt charged into the tanks late in 1964.
Prenuclear testing, including 1100 hr of salt circulation, occupied January-May, 1965.
During nuclear startup experiments in May-July, 1965, fuel salt was circulated for 800 hr.
The salt was drained and final preparations for power operations were made in the fall of 1965.

This included INOR-8 pieces from a control rod thimble, the heat exchanger shell and tubes, the pump bowl cage and baffle, and a freeze valve.
The nuclear fuel was $33\%$ -enriched $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , and the $\mathrm{UF_4}$ concentration in the fuel salt was 0.8 mole $\%$ until 1968.
Then the uranium was removed by fluorination and $^{233}\mathrm{UF_4}$ was substituted.
The $\mathrm{UF_4}$ concentration required with $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ was only 0.13 mole $\%$ .

The composition of the fuel salt was observed by frequent sampling from the pump bowl.[13] Aside from the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ loading and periodic additions of small increments of uranium or plutonium to sustain the nuclear reactivity, the only other additions to the fuel salt were more or less routine small ( $\sim 10\mathrm{g}$ ) quantities of beryllium, and, in two or three experiments, a few grams of zirconium and $\mathrm{FeF_2}$ .

INOR-8 specimens removed from the core and from the control facility were subjected to a variety of examinations and tests.
Metallographic examination of selected specimens was used to give more information on the compatibility of INOR-8 with the salt environment.
Creep tests to determine creep rates, rupture life, and rupture strain were conducted at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and stress levels from 17,000 to 55,000 psi.
Tensile tests giving yield stress, ultimate stress, and fracture strain were made at temperatures from 25 to $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Selected samples that had been tensile tested at 25 and at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were examined metallographically.

The important point to be noted here is that INOR-8 specimens exposed to fuel salt for a considerable period of time before the generation of substantial nuclear power (and fission-product inventories) showed no in-intergranular surface cracks upon testing at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Ultimate tensile stresses decreased significantly from the unexposed specimens to the control specimens to the core specimens, as shown in Table 4.

(INOR-8 tensile specimens tested at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ continue to strain-harden to near fracture, so any reduction in fracture strain would cause failure at a lower ultimate stress.) Examination of these and later specimens indicated that the reduction in fracture strain at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was not connected with the surface-cracking phenomenon, but was due to carbide precipitation that occurred upon aging and was enhanced by irradiation.

All of the INOR-8 specimens exposed to the cell atmosphere outside the reactor vessel $(\mathbf{N}_2$ with $2 - 5\%$ $\mathsf{O}_2)$ developed a dark gray-green, tenacious surface film.
Figure 54 is a photomicrograph of one of the cell specimens exposed the longest (17,483 hr at high temperature).
It shows a very thin, uniform layer of oxide on the surface, with internal oxidation extending to a depth of 1 to 2 mils.

Results of $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ tensile tests on the standard INOR-8 specimens (heats 5065 and 5085) showed reductions (relative to unexposed material) in ultimate stress and fracture strain consistent with expectations from the results on control and core specimens.
Figure 55 shows a sample of heat 5065 exposed to the cell environment and fractured at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The elongated grains attest to the large fracture strain (59%), and the fracture is mixed transgranular and intergranular.
A heat 5085 specimen exposed the same length of time failed with only 33% fracture strain, in a primarily intergranular mode as shown in Fig.

A major objective of the postoperation examination of the MSRE $^{19,20}$ was to supplement the findings on the INOR-8 surveillance specimens by examining pieces of INOR-8 from different parts of the fuel system.
All parts had been at high temperature for 31,000 hr and in contact with fuel salt for 21,000 hr (about 1.5 times as long as any surveillance specimen).

A control rod thimble (which had seen the highest neutron fluence of any INOR-8 ever examined) offered three kinds of surface exposure on the same piece: the inside exposed to $\mathbf{N}_2 + 2\%$ $\mathbf{O}_2$ at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , outside surface exposed to flowing fuel salt, and outside surface that had been under a loose-fitting INOR-8 sleeve.
The MSRE used three control rods<sup>21</sup> fabricated of $\mathrm{Gd}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ canned in Inconel 600.
The control rods operated inside INOR-8 thimbles made of 2-in.-OD × 0.065-in.-wall tubing.

Trauger and Conlin ran two INOR-8 pump loops in the MTR.[24,25] These operated at a peak temperature of $704^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at an average power density of $66\mathrm{W/cm}^3$ .
The loops both contained salt of composition[7] $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - UF_4}$ (62-37-1 mole %).
The first loop ran at a peak Reynolds number of 4100 and operated for 638 hr.
The second loop had a peak Reynolds number of 3100 and operated for 766 hr.
A detailed metallographic examination was made of the first loop.[27] The loop had a nose cone that was closest to the reactor, and it was here that the type of attack shown in Fig.
The attack is actually a surface roughening in which grains were removed from the inside of the INOR-8 tubing.
The second loop did not show this type of attack.[28] The leak in the heat exchanger was not located in either loop.
The fission density in these loops was $66\mathrm{W/cm^3}$ , with salt volumes of $135\mathrm{cm^3}$ and surface areas of about $650\mathrm{cm^2}$ each.
About $1.4 \times 10^{17}$ atoms of Tellurium were produced per unit of metal surface area in the MSRE and only $0.2 \times 10^{17}$ atoms/ $\mathrm{cm^2}$ in these two loops.
A more recent in-reactor thermal convection loop was run by Compere et al.[26] A schematic of the loop is shown in Fig.
106, and the pertinent operating statistics are given in Table 12.

The loop was constructed of INOR-8, contained salt of composition $^{7}\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ (65.3-28.2-4.8-1.7 mole %), and had a graphite region where power densities of $150~\mathrm{W / cm^3}$ of salt were attained.
The loop had maximum and minimum operating temperatures of 720 and $545^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively.
The loop failed at the hottest portion after 1366 hr of nuclear operation.
107) is quite typical of high-temperature failures noted previously in this material after irradiation.[4-7] Note that the inside surface is free of cracks except very near the point of failure.
Surface cracks were very infrequent and extended to a maximum depth of 0.5 mil.
Cracks occurred along almost every grain boundary, but they only extended to a maximum depth of 1 mil.
The amount of tellurium produced in this loop per unit area of metal surface was $4.2 \times 10^{16}$ atoms/cm² (reference 29), compared with $1.4 \times 10^{17}$ atoms/cm² in the MSRE.
Thus, the amount of tellurium was one-fourth that in the test loop, but the system was above $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ only 937 hr when fission products were present.

The experiments fall into the general categories of (1) corrosion in salt, (2) exposure to several fission products to compare the tendencies to produce cracks; (3) diffusion of Te, and (4) experiments with an applied stress.
These experiments have involved materials other than INOR-8 in an effort to better understand the cracking phenomenon and to find a material that is more resistant to cracking.
A thermal convection loop containing a fuel salt had operated for about 30,000 hr with a very low corrosion rate.
The oxidation potential of the salt was increased by two additions of 500 ppm $\mathrm{FeF}_2$ , and the specimens were examined

Three types of experiments have been run in which samples are exposed to fission products: (1) exposure of mechanical property samples to vapors of fission products, (2) electroplating tellurium on mechanical property samples, and (3) studying the mechanical properties of alloys that contain small alloying additions of the fission products.
These materials have sufficient vapor pressure at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to transport to mechanical property samples.

The samples were annealed in a quartz capsule with each fission product for various times at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , deformed at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and sectioned for metallographic examination.

Of the samples of INOR-8, type 304L stainless steel, and nickel-200 exposed to all seven elements for 2000 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , only INOR-8 and nickel 200 exposed to tellurium had intergranular cracks after deformation at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Exposure to tellurium over the temperature range of 550 to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and concentration range of two orders of magnitude showed that the severity of cracking in INOR-8 and nickel 200 increased with increasing temperature and tellurium concentration.

One set of samples was annealed for 1000 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the other set was annealed 200 hr at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The materials included consisted of (1) iron and several stainless steels, (2) nickel and several nickel-base alloys, (3) copper and monel, (4) two cobalt-base alloys, and (5) several heats of INOR-8 with modified chemical compositions.
The samples were deformed at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and examined metal-lographically to determine whether cracks were present.

Nickel, Hastelloy B (1% Maximum Cr), Hastelloy W (5% Cr), and INOR-8 (7% Cr) formed cracks, but several alloys containing 15% or more Cr did not crack (e.g., Inconel 600, Hastelloy C, and Hastelloy X).
Small melts have been made containing nominal additions of $0.01\%$ each of S, Se, Te, Sr, Tc, Ru, Sn, Sb, and As.
Tests on these alloys show no measurable effects on their mechanical properties at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The rate of diffusion of tellurium in INOR-8, nickel 200, and type 304L stainless steel has been measured.

The penetration depths were quite small in some samples, but the data generally give the bulk and grain boundary diffusion coefficients at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ accurately enough to make predictions of the depth of penetration in service.
The depth was very shallow in type 304L stainless steel, at least twice as deep in INOR-8, and many times greater in nickel 200.

The Fisher model for grain boundary diffusion relates the depth of penetration to the diffusion time to the one-fourth power.[38] Thus, the increase in the depth of penetration for a system at temperature for 30 years (an MSBR) is only 1.8 times as great as that for a system at temperatures for three years (the MSRE).
